nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; generally found on exposed benches and seaward reef faces. Has been documented behaving secretively in reef structures, with activity sometimes observed at night. Depth range spans 0–64 m, with typical occurrence between 1–20 m.

decorPreferences: dense live rock with caves and crevices sized to conceal the full body
tight-fitting, weighted lid with no gaps

diet.notes: Primarily a fish predator in the wild. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen meaty foods such as silversides, krill, whole shrimp, squid, and chopped fish. Newly acquired individuals may initially require live prey (ghost shrimp, small feeder fish) before transitioning to prepared or frozen foods. Feeding with long tongs or a feeding stick is generally advised; the species has poor eyesight and has been known to bite by accident. Adults are typically fed two to three times per week.

goodWith: large, robust fish not small enough to be swallowed
avoidWith: all crustaceans — shrimp, crabs, hermit crabs, lobsters (natural prey)
other moray eels unless tank is very large and both introduced simultaneously

summary: A rare Indo-Pacific moray eel reaching up to ~70 cm, found on exposed seaward reef faces across a wide range from the Red Sea to French Polynesia. Generally considered more challenging to source than common moray species; care requirements are consistent with other medium-large Muraenidae.
careNotes:
Enchelycore bayeri is considered a relatively uncommon species in the aquarium trade, with FishBase noting it as 'a rare species' in the wild. Its care requirements are broadly consistent with other medium-to-large moray eels in the family Muraenidae.

A tightly sealed, weighted aquarium lid is considered essential — moray eels are widely documented as escape artists capable of pushing through loose-fitting covers. All gaps should be filled. An efficient protein skimmer and robust biological filtration are generally advised given the carnivore's heavy bioload.

The species is generally not considered reef-safe: it will prey on any crustacean or small fish sharing the tank. Tankmates should be limited to large, robust fish that cannot fit in the eel's mouth.

This species has been noted to be nocturnal or crepuscular in the wild, remaining secretive in reef crevices during the day. Feeding is best attempted at dusk or after lights-off in the first weeks of acclimation. FishBase notes 'may bite by accident or when provoked'; feeding with a long stick or tongs is strongly recommended.

No dedicated aquarium care source was found specifically for Enchelycore bayeri (LiveAquaria, BlueZoo, and Reef2Reef did not have a species page for this eel at the time of research). Water parameters, care level, and tank size were derived by cross-referencing FishBase wild data (temperature 24.9–28.9°C; Indo-Pacific reef habitat) with established LiveAquaria care profiles for related Muraenidae species of comparable size (Jeweled Moray Eel at 61 cm → 300 L; Yellow-Head Moray at 76–93 cm → 500 L). The 400 L minimum was selected from the nice-values list as the cautious rounded figure between these two comparators, reflecting this species' 70 cm maximum size and its need for territorial rockwork caves.

Temperature range was derived by averaging FishBase wild-range data (24.9–28.9°C) with LiveAquaria's standard saltwater moray recommendation (72–78°F = 22.2–25.6°C), yielding an averaged range of approximately 23.5–27.2°C, rounded to 24–27°C. This is consistent with published ranges for other Indo-Pacific Muraenidae kept in reef aquariums.

Salinity and pH values (SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12) are standard Indo-Pacific reef parameters, consistent across all LiveAquaria moray eel care profiles reviewed and broadly appropriate for the species' wild habitat.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented in the sources reviewed. As with most moray eels, captive reproduction is not considered achievable under typical home aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria does not have a dedicated species page for Enchelycore bayeri. Care parameters were extrapolated from LiveAquaria profiles for comparable Muraenidae species: Jeweled Moray Eel (Muraena lentiginosa, 61 cm, 300 L minimum) and Yellow-Head Moray Eel (Gymnothorax fimbriatus, 76–93 cm, 500 L minimum). Water parameters (SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, temp 72–78°F) are consistent across all LiveAquaria moray profiles.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Primary source for taxonomy, adult size (max 70 cm TL, common 61 cm), native range (Indo-Pacific: Indian Ocean incl. Red Sea to French Polynesia; absent Hawaiian Islands), preferred temperature range (24.9–28.9°C), and habitat description (seaward reef benches; secretive; sometimes nocturnal). No salinity or pH data provided. IUCN status: Least Concern.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented in reviewed sources.
